questions: [{"answer":1,"options":["Mutation","Natural selection","Genetic drift","Gene flow"],"question":"Industrial melanism in Biston betularia moth is an example of:","explanation":"Industrial melanism in peppered moths (Biston betularia) is a classic example of natural selection. The dark (melanic) form became predominant in industrial areas where pollution killed lichens and blackened tree trunks, making light moths more visible to predators. This demonstrates directional natural selection in action."},{"answer":2,"options":["Homologous organs","Vestigial organs","Analogous organs","Fossils"],"question":"Which of the following is NOT evidence for evolution?","explanation":"Analogous organs (e.g., wings of birds and insects) are similar in function but different in origin and structure—they indicate convergent evolution, not common ancestry. Homologous organs, vestigial organs, and fossils are direct evidence of evolution from common ancestors."},{"answer":1,"options":["Sympatric speciation","Allopatric speciation","Parapatric speciation","Polyploidy"],"question":"Speciation that occurs due to geographic isolation is called:","explanation":"Allopatric speciation occurs when populations are physically separated by geographic barriers (mountains, rivers), preventing gene flow. Over time, genetic divergence leads to new species. Sympatric speciation occurs without geographic isolation, within the same area."},{"answer":1,"options":["Platyhelminthes","Apicomplexa (Sporozoa)","Sarcomastigophora","Ciliophora"],"question":"Plasmodium belongs to which phylum?","explanation":"Plasmodium (the malaria parasite) belongs to Phylum Apicomplexa (formerly Sporozoa). It is characterized by an apical complex used for cell invasion, non-motile adults, and obligatory intracellular parasitism. It has no locomotory organelles in the adult stage."},{"answer":1,"options":["Bilateral symmetry","Notochord, dorsal hollow nerve cord, pharyngeal slits","Exoskeleton","Triploblastic body plan"],"question":"Which feature is unique to Phylum Chordata?","explanation":"All chordates possess three unique features at some stage: notochord (supportive rod), dorsal hollow nerve cord, and pharyngeal slits (gill slits). These features may be transient (only in embryo) or permanent. Bilateral symmetry and triploblastic body plans are found in many other phyla too."},{"answer":1,"options":["Earthworm","Cockroach","Frog","Human"],"question":"Open circulatory system is found in:","explanation":"Cockroach (and most insects) have an open circulatory system where hemolymph (blood) flows freely in body cavities (hemocoel) and is not confined to blood vessels. Earthworms, frogs, and humans have closed circulatory systems where blood stays within vessels."},{"answer":1,"options":["Annelida","Platyhelminthes","Arthropoda","Echinodermata"],"question":"Flame cells are excretory structures found in:","explanation":"Flame cells (protonephridia) are the excretory units of Platyhelminthes (flatworms). They consist of hollow cup-shaped cells with cilia that create a flickering flame-like appearance. They regulate water and excrete waste by osmoregulation."},{"answer":1,"options":["Merozoite","Sporozoite","Gametocyte","Trophozoite"],"question":"The infective stage of Plasmodium for humans (transmitted by Anopheles mosquito) is:","explanation":"Sporozoites are injected into humans through the saliva of infected female Anopheles mosquito during a blood meal. They travel to the liver and begin the exo-erythrocytic cycle. Merozoites infect RBCs, gametocytes are taken up by mosquitoes, and trophozoites are feeding stages in RBCs."},{"answer":2,"options":["Lungs","Gills","Moist skin (cutaneous respiration)","Trachea"],"question":"Earthworm respires through:","explanation":"Earthworms lack lungs, gills, or trachea. They perform cutaneous (skin) respiration—oxygen and CO2 diffuse through the moist body wall. The skin must remain moist for gas exchange, which is why earthworms die when exposed to dry conditions."},{"answer":1,"options":["Flame cells","Nephridia","Malpighian tubules","Green glands"],"question":"The excretory organs of earthworm are:","explanation":"Earthworms excrete through nephridia (similar to kidneys). Each segment (except first 3 and last) contains a pair of nephridia. They remove nitrogenous wastes (mainly urea and ammonia) and regulate water balance. Malpighian tubules are in insects; flame cells in flatworms."},{"answer":2,"options":["Vision","Smell","Hearing","Balance"],"question":"Tympanum in frog is associated with:","explanation":"The tympanum (eardrum) in frogs is a circular membrane located behind the eye. It vibrates in response to sound waves and transmits them to the inner ear for hearing. It infects all ages of red blood cells, causes high parasitemia, and can lead to organ failure. P. vivax causes relapsing malaria but is rarely fatal. P. malariae causes quartan malaria."},{"answer":2,"options":["AV node","Bundle of His","SA node","Purkinje fibers"],"question":"The pacemaker of the heart is the:","explanation":"The Sinoatrial (SA) node located in the right atrium wall is the heart's natural pacemaker. Conversely, in the lungs, low CO2 and higher pH increase O2 binding."},{"answer":2,"options":["Loop of Henle","Collecting duct","Bowman's capsule + glomerulus","Distal convoluted tubule"],"question":"Filtration occurs in which part of the nephron?","explanation":"Glomerular filtration occurs at the renal corpuscle (Bowman's capsule + glomerulus). This forms the primary urine (filtrate)."},{"answer":1,"options":["Alpha cells","Beta cells","Delta cells","Acinar cells"],"question":"Insulin is secreted by which cells of the pancreas?","explanation":"Beta (β) cells of the islets of Langerhans in the pancreas secrete insulin in response to high blood glucose. Myelin insulates axons, increases conduction velocity through saltatory conduction, and reduces energy use. Demyelination causes conditions like multiple sclerosis."},{"answer":1,"options":["Memory and learning","Coordination, balance, and fine motor control","Regulation of heartbeat","Processing of sensory information"],"question":"The cerebellum is primarily responsible for:","explanation":"The cerebellum coordinates voluntary movement, maintains balance and posture, and fine-tunes motor activity. During contraction, I bands and H zones shorten while A bands remain constant (sliding filament theory)."},{"answer":2,"options":["Glucose reabsorption","Protein filtration","Concentration of urine (creating osmotic gradient)","Urea synthesis"],"question":"The main function of the Loop of Henle is:","explanation":"The Loop of Henle creates a countercurrent multiplier system that establishes an osmotic gradient in the renal medulla. They are excellent bioindicators of air quality."},{"answer":1,"options":["Secondary succession","Primary succession","Seral succession","Climax community"],"question":"Ecological succession that begins in a previously uninhabited area (bare rock) is called:","explanation":"Primary succession begins on bare, lifeless substrates (bare rock, lava flows, sand dunes) where no soil exists. It reduces biodiversity and water quality."},{"answer":1,"options":["Chemical energy","Solar energy (sunlight)","Geothermal energy","Wind energy"],"question":"Which is the ultimate source of energy in most ecosystems?","explanation":"Solar energy is the ultimate source of energy for most ecosystems on Earth. This creates two single-stranded templates for replication. SSB proteins keep the strands apart, and DNA gyrase/topoisomerase relieves torsional stress ahead of helicase."},{"answer":1,"options":["All traits are inherited together","Two alleles for each trait separate so that each gamete carries only one allele","Dominant alleles suppress recessive ones in gametes","Traits blend in the offspring"],"question":"According to Mendel's Law of Segregation, during gamete formation:","explanation":"Mendel's First Law (Law of Segregation): The two alleles for a character segregate during gamete formation (meiosis), so each gamete carries only one allele. Upon fertilization, the zygote gets one allele from each parent, restoring the diploid state. This explains the 3:1 ratio in F2 monohybrid cross."},{"answer":1,"options":["Homozygous dominant","Heterozygous","Homozygous recessive","Cannot be determined"],"question":"In a test cross, a plant showing dominant phenotype is crossed with homozygous recessive. The ratio obtained is 1:1. This means the test plant is:","explanation":"A test cross reveals the genotype of a dominant phenotype individual. If the ratio is 1:1 (dominant:recessive), the test plant must be heterozygous (Aa × aa → 1 Aa : 1 aa). If 100% show dominant phenotype, the test plant is homozygous dominant (AA × aa → all Aa)."},{"answer":1,"options":["Linkage between genes","Independent assortment of two genes on different chromosomes","Incomplete dominance","Co-dominance"],"question":"The dihybrid ratio of 9:3:3:1 assumes:","explanation":"The 9:3:3:1 phenotypic ratio in F2 of a dihybrid cross (AaBb × AaBb) occurs when two genes assort independently (Mendel's Second Law). This is possible when the genes are on different chromosomes (non-homologous). Linked genes deviate from this ratio."},{"answer":2,"options":["Carry the genetic code from DNA to ribosome","Serve as a template for protein synthesis","Carry specific amino acids to the ribosome during translation","Catalyze peptide bond formation"],"question":"tRNA functions to:","explanation":"Transfer RNA (tRNA) acts as an adaptor molecule. It carries specific amino acids to the ribosome during translation. Each tRNA has an anticodon (3 nucleotides) that base-pairs with the codon on mRNA, ensuring the correct amino acid is incorporated into the growing polypeptide chain."},{"answer":2,"options":["DNA → Protein → RNA","RNA → DNA → Protein","DNA → RNA → Protein","Protein → RNA → DNA"],"question":"The Central Dogma of Molecular Biology states:","explanation":"The Central Dogma (proposed by Francis Crick, 1958) describes the flow of genetic information: DNA is transcribed into mRNA (transcription), which is translated into protein (translation). Reverse transcriptase in retroviruses can flow RNA→DNA. Information cannot generally flow from protein back to nucleic acid."},{"answer":1,"options":["Start of translation","End of translation (termination)","Addition of a specific amino acid","Switching to different ribosome"],"question":"Stop codons (UAA, UAG, UGA) on mRNA signal:","explanation":"Stop codons (UAA = ochre, UAG = amber, UGA = opal/umber) signal the termination of translation. No standard tRNA anticodon matches them; instead, release factors recognize stop codons and promote release of the polypeptide and disassembly of the ribosome."},{"answer":2,"options":["Protein synthesis","Photosynthesis","Cellular respiration (ATP synthesis via oxidative phosphorylation)","Lipid synthesis"],"question":"The powerhouse of the cell, mitochondria, is the site of:","explanation":"Mitochondria are the site of aerobic cellular respiration. The Krebs cycle occurs in the mitochondrial matrix, while the electron transport chain (ETC) and oxidative phosphorylation occur on the inner mitochondrial membrane, producing the bulk of ATP (~28-32 ATP per glucose)."},{"answer":1,"options":["Apical meristem","Lateral meristem (vascular cambium and cork cambium)","Intercalary meristem","Ground meristem"],"question":"Secondary growth in dicot stems is caused by activity of:","explanation":"Secondary growth (increase in girth) in dicots occurs due to lateral meristems: vascular cambium (produces secondary xylem/phloem) and cork cambium/phellogen (produces bark). Monocots generally lack vascular cambium and don't show secondary growth."},{"answer":1,"options":["Water absorption by roots","Apoplastic movement of water into the vascular cylinder","Transpiration from leaves","Nutrient absorption by roots"],"question":"The Casparian strip in the root endodermis prevents:","explanation":"The Casparian strip is a band of suberin (waxy substance) in the radial and transverse walls of endodermal cells. It blocks the apoplastic (through cell walls) pathway of water and solutes, forcing them through the symplastic (through cytoplasm) pathway where selective ion uptake can occur."},{"answer":2,"options":["Root pressure","Capillary action alone","Cohesion of water molecules and tension created by transpiration","Active transport by xylem cells"],"question":"The cohesion-tension theory (transpiration pull) explains water movement in xylem through:","explanation":"The Cohesion-Tension theory (Dixon and Joly) explains upward movement of water. Transpiration from leaves creates tension/negative pressure (pulling). Water molecules cohere (hydrogen bonds) to form a continuous column from roots to leaves. Adhesion to xylem walls also helps. This can pull water up to 130m in tall trees."},{"answer":1,"options":["Xylem vessel loading","Sieve tube elements, aiding in phloem loading and transport","Providing structural support","Water storage"],"question":"Companion cells in phloem are associated with:","explanation":"Companion cells are specialized parenchyma cells intimately connected to sieve tube elements (anucleate) via plasmodesmata. They assist in active loading/unloading of sugars (especially sucrose) into phloem."},{"answer":1,"options":["C3 plants (Calvin cycle)","C4 plants (Hatch-Slack pathway)","CAM plants","Both C3 and C4 plants"],"question":"Oxaloacetic acid (OAA) is the first stable product of CO2 fixation in:","explanation":"In C4 plants (sugarcane, maize, sorghum), CO2 is first fixed in mesophyll cells by PEP carboxylase to form oxaloacetate (OAA, 4-carbon compound). This is unique to flowering plants (angiosperms)."},{"answer":1,"options":["Vitamin C","Beta-carotene (precursor to Vitamin A)","Increased protein content","Insect resistance"],"question":"Golden Rice is genetically engineered to produce:","explanation":"Golden Rice is a transgenic variety engineered to produce beta-carotene (provitamin A) in the endosperm, giving it a golden color. The genes were sourced from daffodil and a bacterium."},{"answer":1,"options":["Produces growth hormones","Fixes atmospheric nitrogen in legume root nodules","Decomposes organic matter","Produces phosphate"],"question":"Rhizobium is an important biofertilizer because it:","explanation":"Rhizobium is a symbiotic nitrogen-fixing bacterium that lives in root nodules of legumes (peas, beans, soybean, clover).
